title: Plain-language human question model
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Redesign the Human action queue copy and row model so it represents explicit questions agents ask a human, not coordinator merge-review workload. Prefer clear title, why it matters, what answer is needed, and short code. Keep the UI read-only.

title: Human question empty and sample states
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Improve empty states and sample/fallback rendering so a human sees either real open questions or a calm explanation that no one has asked them anything yet. Do not show internal needs-port items as human questions.

title: Copy-code affordance polish
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Make the short-code copy affordance obvious but minimal. Consider copied feedback that does not mutate swarm state, keyboard focus, button text, and screen-reader labels. Keep it read-only.

title: Question lane context
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Make lane filtering understandable for human questions: when a lane is selected, show only questions actually scoped to that lane and explain when workspace-wide questions are hidden or shown.

title: Answered/resolved lifecycle semantics
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Review how answered/resolved/dismissed human questions should disappear from the read-only queue. Patch client filtering and tests if needed so coordinator-consumed answers are no longer shown.

args: cd frontier-loom-ui && npm run typecheck && cd ../frontier-swarm-codex && npm run typecheck

title: Human question user-story review
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Review the Actions tab as a human user. Patch wording or layout only where it directly makes questions easier to answer in chat. Avoid adding controls or settings.

title: Task-first information architecture
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Reassess the top-level hierarchy from a human operator perspective: goal, epics/tasks, actual human questions, quality/testing, cost/performance, semantic merge, cadence, evidence. Patch labels/order/details if they still feel AI-internal.

title: Work overview reduction
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Reduce the Work tab to the smallest useful human overview. Avoid repeating telemetry from other tabs. Keep epics/tasks and next true human questions prominent.

title: Mobile legibility and overflow
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Improve mobile and narrow desktop legibility for tabs, lane rail, action rows, and metric cards. Do not introduce dense new content. Run or describe a browser overflow check.

title: Minimal color and chrome pass
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Audit CSS color/chrome intensity. Keep the dashboard dark, calm, and minimal. Patch excessive borders, accent stripes, or competing status colors only if they reduce readability.

title: Raw evidence containment
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Keep raw jobs/events/sources available but clearly secondary. Patch Evidence tab wording/layout if it still reads like the primary dashboard.

title: Charting library fit research
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Research current open-source charting libraries suitable for a dark read-only operations dashboard. Compare bundle cost, React/DOM fit, accessibility, tree/graph support, heatmap support, and no-build complexity. Write concise findings; patch production only if very low risk.

title: Semantic merge graph visualization research
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Research lightweight ways to visualize semantic merge/version trees: CSS/SVG, vis-network, cytoscape, d3-dag, mermaid-like syntax, or custom DOM. Recommend what fits the dashboard and why.

title: Cadence heatmap research
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Research GitHub-contribution-style heatmap options for cadence/progress: CSS grid, calendar-heatmap libraries, d3, uPlot, Observable Plot. Recommend a minimal path for this package.

title: Native CSS chart prototype
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Prototype, if low risk, a tiny native DOM/CSS cadence or merge visualization improvement without adding dependencies. Otherwise write why a dependency is needed.

title: Human action schema contract
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Refine FrontierCodexDashboardHumanAction so it models agent-authored human questions: prompt, context, requested answer shape, options/choices, status, answer/resolution, and source refs. Keep it additive and typed.

title: Board entry to human question mapping
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Review mapping from board-like entries to dashboard human actions. Ensure only explicit review-question/escalation/decision entries intended for humans become human questions; needs-port worker output should not automatically become a human question.

title: Answer and resolution tests
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Add or propose smoke tests proving answered/resolved human questions are hidden from open queues while unresolved explicit questions remain visible in the snapshot.

title: Browser verification of human queue
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Use the existing UI and tests to verify the human question queue is legible on desktop/mobile and does not show coordinator-internal merge-review rows as human requests. Patch smoke checks if useful.

title: Adversarial product review
objective: You are not alone in the codebase; do not revert other changes. Review the whole dashboard as a human-facing product. Identify whether the page answers: what is happening, what needs me, what changed, what is risky, what is next. Patch only focused issues; otherwise leave exact findings.
